w: 'At sixes and sevens' comes directly from here — Skinners and Merchant Taylors have disputed sixth place in the livery order since the Lord Mayor's arbitration of 1484, alternating annually ever since. William Jupp's hall of 1770 replaced three predecessors. Oldest unsettled argument in the City.
_facts: The expression 'at sixes and sevens' derives from the dispute between Skinners and Merchant Taylors over livery precedence
The Lord Mayor arbitrated in 1484, ordering the two companies to alternate sixth and seventh place each year
The current hall was designed by William Jupp and built 1770-90
The company received its first royal charter in 1327

s: Animal-fat candle guild, Dowgate Hill since 1462
w: Before gas and electricity, London ran on tallow — rendered animal fat, shaped into candles by this guild. Their hall on Dowgate Hill has stood since a royal charter of 1462, one of the oldest continuously occupied livery sites in the City. The company now tests lubricants. Candlemakers turned oil inspectors.
_facts: The Tallow Chandlers' Company received its royal charter in 1462
Tallow was the primary candle material before gas and electric lighting
The hall on Dowgate Hill is among the oldest continuously occupied livery sites in the City
The company today tests and certifies lubricating oils and greases

s: Two nicks mark a royal swan
w: One of only three bodies allowed to own mute swans on the Thames, the Vintners mark theirs with two nicks on the beak at the annual Swan Upping. Their hall dates to 1446 and the wine cellar holds around 16,000 bottles. Royal privilege, carved into every swan's bill. Still.
_facts: The Vintners' Company is one of only three bodies permitted to own mute swans on the Thames
During Swan Upping, Vintners' swans are identified by two nicks on the beak
Vintners' Hall on Upper Thames Street dates to 1446
The hall's wine cellar holds approximately 16,000 bottles

w: Built in 1893 for the Institute of Chartered Accountants, this Neo-Baroque pile contains the only indoor bridge in the City — a glass-roofed loggia between two wings. Hamo Thornycroft carved the friezes of Commerce and Industry outside. Extended in 1930 and 1970. Accountancy's most theatrical address.
_facts: The hall was built in 1893 by John Belcher in Neo-Baroque style
It contains the only indoor bridge in the City of London
Hamo Thornycroft created allegorical friezes for the exterior
The building was extended in 1930 and again in 1970

s: Elizabeth I named it; proclamations still read from the steps
w: Thomas Gresham founded it in 1571; Elizabeth I visited and granted it the name herself. The current William Tite colonnade dates to 1844 after two earlier buildings burned. Leighton murals inside; royal proclamations still read from the steps. Boutiques where merchants once haggled.
_facts: Sir Thomas Gresham founded the Royal Exchange in 1565-71, modelled on the Antwerp Bourse
Queen Elizabeth I granted it the name 'Royal Exchange' in 1571
The current building was designed by William Tite and opened in 1844
Frederick Leighton painted allegorical murals in the interior
Royal proclamations are traditionally read from the steps

s: 72 miles of London records, back to 1067
w: The largest local government record office in the UK, holding London's administrative memory from 1067 to last week — 72 miles of shelving in a former print works on Northampton Road. Parish registers, maps, photographs, court records: all freely searchable. London's memory in a Clerkenwell printshop.
_facts: London Metropolitan Archives holds approximately 72 miles of shelving, the largest local authority record office in the UK
Holdings date back to 1067
The archive occupies a former printing works on Northampton Road, Clerkenwell
Access to the archives and reading rooms is free

s: Art Deco pool where Eastern Promises was filmed
w: Built 1931, extended to Art Deco glory in 1938, this Islington baths survived the Blitz and near-closure before restoration in 2012. The Turkish baths still hold original marble slabs. Cronenberg shot the fight scene from Eastern Promises in the pool. Working-class institution.
_facts: Ironmonger Row Baths opened in 1931 and were extended in Art Deco style in 1938
The Turkish baths retain their original marble slabs
David Cronenberg filmed the pool fight scene from Eastern Promises (2007) here
The baths were restored and reopened in 2012

s: Opened 1939, closed 4 months later for a war
w: The last municipal lido built in London opened in June 1939 and closed that October, requisitioned before most Londoners had swum a length. It sat derelict for decades — storage, army training ground — before a community campaign forced its restoration. Reopened 2013. Seventy years between dips.
_facts: Charlton Lido was the last municipal outdoor lido built in London, opening June 1939
It was requisitioned by the military four months after opening, in October 1939
After decades of dereliction it was restored through a community campaign
The restored lido reopened in 2013

s: Rennie's granite vaults held 8 million gallons of wine
w: John Rennie built this in 1812 as the most secure warehouse in the world: underground granite vaults holding 8 million gallons of wine, prison-like security to deter endemic dockland theft. The tobacco came later. Grade I listed. The 1980s shopping mall failed inside two years. Rennie's vaults endure.
_facts: Tobacco Dock was designed by John Rennie and completed in 1812
The underground granite vaults were designed to hold 8 million gallons of wine and spirits
Extreme security features combated endemic theft in the London docks
It is Grade I listed
A retail complex opened in 1989 but failed within two years

s: 960 yards, no towpath — bargees walked the ceiling
w: London's longest canal tunnel runs 960 yards under Islington. No towpath: bargees 'legged' their boats through by lying on the roof and walking the tunnel ceiling with their feet, while the horse was led over the hill above. Waymarker stones on the surface still trace that overland route.
_facts: The Islington Tunnel is 960 yards (878m) long, the longest canal tunnel in London
There was no towpath, so boatmen 'legged' vessels by walking their feet along the tunnel ceiling
The horse pulling the barge was led over Islington Hill via the surface route
Waymarker stones on the surface trace the horses' overland route

w: Specialist in electronic, dance and experimental music since 2003, Phonica has become one of Europe's most respected independent shops — stocking releases most shops never carry, hosting in-stores for The Vinyl Factory and Ninja Tune. The listening posts are the point. You discover music by being here.
_facts: Phonica Records was founded in 2003 on Poland Street, Soho
It specialises in electronic, dance and experimental music
The shop is closely associated with The Vinyl Factory label and hosts in-store sessions
Widely regarded as one of Europe's leading independent record stores for electronic music

s: The only antiquarian bookseller on Bond Street
w: Since 1979, Bernard Shapero has run the only antiquarian bookshop on Bond Street — a street otherwise given entirely to watches and handbags. Stock runs from medieval manuscripts to first-edition travel and antique maps. One of the world's leading dealers in early cartography. A library tucked into a jewellery box.
_facts: Shapero Rare Books was founded by Bernard Shapero in 1979
It is the only antiquarian bookshop on Bond Street, Mayfair
The shop specialises in rare books, antique maps, atlases and manuscripts
Recognised internationally as a leading dealer in early cartography and travel literature

w: 'Books' backwards, underground in the Brunswick Centre's basement — Skoob is Bloomsbury's secondhand institution, with over 70,000 titles across every subject. Moved below ground in 2008 after 25 years above. The philosophy section fills a wall. Time Out's best London bookshop, 2010. You'll lose an afternoon.
_facts: Skoob Books takes its name from 'books' spelled backwards
The shop stocks over 70,000 secondhand and remaindered titles
It moved to the Brunswick Centre basement in 2008
Time Out named it the best bookshop in London in 2010

s: London's oldest surviving street of houses
w: Numbers 41 and 42 are the oldest domestic houses in the City — around 1614, pre-dating the Great Fire by 52 years, timber-framed upper storeys intact. Betjeman lived at no.43 and called it his favourite London street. The cloth fair ran here from 1133 to 1855. Gates shut nightly until 1910.
_facts: 41 and 42 Cloth Fair are the oldest surviving domestic buildings in the City of London, dating to around 1614
Poet laureate John Betjeman lived at 43 Cloth Fair and called it his favourite London street
Bartholomew Fair was held here from 1133 until 1855
Gates were closed nightly until 1910

s: Last intact Elizabethan street facade in London
w: London's only surviving Inn of Chancery: the timber-framed black-and-white facade on High Holborn dates to 1585, one of the last intact Elizabethan frontages in the city. Survived the Great Fire and the Blitz. Johnson wrote Rasselas in one week in a room here to pay for his mother's funeral.
_facts: Staple Inn's timber-framed facade on High Holborn dates to 1585
It is the only surviving Inn of Chancery in London still close to its original form
The building survived the Great Fire of London (1666) and World War II
Samuel Johnson is said to have written Rasselas here in one week in 1759 to pay for his mother's funeral

s: Hawksmoor pyramid topped with George I in a toga
w: Hawksmoor's last London church (1716-31) is also his strangest: the steeple is a stepped pyramid after the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus, and on top — instead of a cross — stands King George I dressed as a Roman emperor. Hogarth mocked it in 'Gin Lane'. He had a point.
_facts: St George's Bloomsbury was designed by Hawksmoor and built 1716-31, his last London church
The steeple is modelled on the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
A statue of King George I in Roman dress stands at the top
William Hogarth depicted the church in 'Gin Lane' (1751) as a symbol of Hanoverian excess

s: Underground HQ whose sculptures caused a national scandal
w: Charles Holden's 1929 London Underground headquarters was the tallest steel-framed office in London at completion. Jacob Epstein's 'Day' and 'Night' on the exterior so shocked the press that the Underground chairman resigned. The sculptures stayed. Grade I listed. Still there.
_facts: 55 Broadway was designed by Charles Holden, completed 1929, London Underground headquarters
At completion it was the tallest steel-framed office building in London
Jacob Epstein's 'Day' and 'Night' sculptures on the exterior caused a public scandal
The controversy led to the resignation of the Underground chairman
The building is Grade I listed

s: Black vitrolite and chrome — the 'Black Lubyanka'
w: The 1932 Daily Express building clad in black vitrolite glass and chrome — London's first fully curtain-walled building — was nicknamed the 'Black Lubyanka' by rival papers. Its streamlined facade conceals a gilded Art Deco foyer with Eric Aumonier relief sculptures. Now Goldman Sachs. The lobby is occasionally open.
_facts: The Daily Express Building at 120 Fleet Street was completed in 1932, designed by Ellis & Clarke with Owen Williams
The facade is clad in black vitrolite with chrome strips — London's first fully curtain-walled building
Nicknamed the 'Black Lubyanka' by rival newspapers
The Art Deco foyer features gilded relief sculptures by Eric Aumonier
Now occupied by Goldman Sachs
